slides.title: Functions That Remember
slides.body: A **closure** is an inner function that *remembers* variables from its outer function — even after that outer function has finished running. Think of it as a function carrying a little backpack of values wherever it goes.
slides.tip: Closures are the secret engine behind decorators and factory functions.

slides.title: Nest a Function, Return It
slides.body: Define a function **inside** another function, then `return` it (without calling it). The returned function keeps the outer variables alive.
slides.tip: Return greet — not greet(). Parentheses call it; no parentheses returns it.

slides.title: Factory Functions
slides.body: Call the outer function with **different arguments** to get back different, pre-configured inner functions — each remembering its own captured value.
slides.code:
def make_multiplier(n):
    def multiply(x):
        return x * n
    return multiply

double = make_multiplier(2)
triple = make_multiplier(3)
print(double(5))
print(triple(5))
slides.tip: double and triple are separate closures — each has its own n in its backpack.

slides.title: Keeping State with nonlocal
slides.body: Closures can also **update** captured variables between calls. Use `nonlocal` to tell Python you mean the outer variable, not a new local one.
slides.code:
def make_counter():
    count = 0
    def increment():
        nonlocal count
        count += 1
        return count
    return increment

counter = make_counter()
print(counter())
print(counter())
slides.tip: Without nonlocal, count += 1 crashes — Python thinks you want a brand-new local variable.

slides.title: You've Got Closures!
slides.body: A closure = inner function + captured variables from an outer scope. Use them to build **configurable functions**, keep **lightweight state**, and power **decorators** — all without writing a full class.
slides.tip: Once closures click, decorators feel obvious: they're just a closure that wraps another function.
